note:
在古典音乐的顶级梯队中，小提琴手詹妮弗·柯（Jennifer Koh）绝对是明星。
 凭借令人眼花technique乱的技巧，她摆脱了任何有抱负的茱莉亚（Juilliard）毕业生梦dream以求的职业-与领先的乐队合作出现，录制新作品并在世界上一些最负盛名的舞台上演出。
 现在，已经经历了九个月的蔓延，大多数公众聚会都停止了，并毁掉了表演艺术。看着一年的票房蒸发的Koh女士正在客厅里播放音乐，并收到食品券。
  几乎在经济的每个角落都可以发现痛苦。自从冠状病毒大流行蔓延到美国以来，数以百万计的人失去了工作，数以万计的企业关门大吉。但是，即使在这样的非凡时期，表演艺术和相关领域的损失也令人震惊。
 根据国家艺术基金会的数据，在截至9月的季度中，总体失业率平均为8.5％，其中52％的演员，55％的舞蹈演员和27％的音乐家失业。相比之下，服务生的失业率是27％。厨师占19％；同期，零售销售人员约占13％。
 在许多地区，艺术场所（剧院，俱乐部，表演空间，音乐厅，节日）是最早关门的企业，很可能是最后关门的企业。
 “我担心的是我们不仅失去工作，而且正在失去职业，”纽约美国音乐家联合会Local 802主席亚当·克劳特哈默（Adam Krauthamer）说。他说，由于强制性关闭，该地区7,000名成员中有95％的成员无法定期工作。他说：“这将造成巨大的文化压抑。” 
国会本周批准的用于表演场所和文化机构的价值150亿美元的新刺激性援助，在特朗普总统批评该法案后陷入了困境，并不会很快消除表演者的大规模失业。而且它只会将联邦失业援助延长至3月中旬。
 公众可能将表演者视为A级名人，但大多数人从来没有走过红地毯或颁奖典礼。即使在最佳时期，绝大多数人也无法从好莱坞般的薪水或机构支持中受益。他们每个季节工作一次，从周末到周末或每天工作，从一次演出到下一场演出。
 专职音乐家和歌手的年薪中位数为42,800美元；演员是40,500美元；根据国家艺术基金会的分析，舞者和编舞的费用为$ 36,500。许多艺术家还从事其他工作，以谋生，通常是在餐馆，零售和酒店业—这些工作也枯竭了。
 他们是美国农村，郊区和城市各个角落的地方经济和社区不可或缺的组成部分，并且他们的生活和生活突然消失。
  特里·伯瑞尔（Terry Burrell）说：“我们谈论的是一年的工作即将结束，”他的巡回演出“生气，喧嚣和华丽无耻”被取消了。现在，她和丈夫在亚特兰大住在一起，领取失业保险，并希望自己不必涉足401（k）退休帐户。
 提琴手Linda Jean Stokley和Montana Hobbs是肯塔基二人组的本地蜂蜜的一部分，他说：“我们有韧性，并且习惯于不定期领薪水。”但她说，自3月份以来，几乎没有人甚至支付了合同规定的小笔费用，“有人欠我们75美元，甚至都没有付款。”
  然后是31岁的蒂姆·吴（Tim Wu），他是DJ的歌手和制片人，通常在大学，节日和夜总会里，每年都会以大象表演方式进行大约100场演出。 
他在密歇根州的安阿伯市（Ann Arbor）进行了声音检查，以检查3月中旬纽约关闭时的新节目《外交》。吴先生第二天回到洛杉矶。他的所有其他预订都被取消了-他的大部分收入都被取消了。
 吴先生和像他这样的成千上万的自由职业者并不是唯一受到打击的人。包括好莱坞和出版业在内的更广泛的艺术和文化领域，构成了一个价值8,870亿美元的产业，在美国经济中的份额超过体育，交通，建筑或农业。根据美国经济分析局的数据，该行业支持510万个工资和薪水工作。他们包括代理商，化妆师，发型师，裁缝，管理员，舞台手，引诱者，电工，音响工程师，特许销售商，相机操作员，管理人员，施工人员，设计师，作家，导演等。
 多伦多大学罗特曼管理学院和城市学院的教授理查德·佛罗里达（Richard Florida）说：“如果城市要反弹，那么没有艺术和文化创意的人就不会这样做。”
  今年，塔尔萨（Tulsa）现年33岁的斯蒂芬·西蒙（Steph Simon）终于开始从事嘻哈音乐人的全职工作，此前他从事打扫地毯或接听电话以支付账单的最低工资工作已达十年之久。
 他被选派到德克萨斯州奥斯汀的西南音乐节举行的南方音乐节上演出，在家庭和巡回演出中定期演出，并制作了“小非洲之火”，以纪念1921年白人暴徒屠杀塔尔萨黑人居民。
 西蒙先生说：“这是我在财务上最大的一年，”西蒙先生与女友和两个女儿住在一起，每月收入约2500美元。他说：“然后世界就关闭了。”
 音乐节取消一周后，他又回到了呼叫中心，当时每周在家工作约40个小时，并在周末在一家快餐店兼职。 
工资单上的表演者也受到了影响。在经历了数年的追赶演出和广告之后，罗宾·克拉克（Robyn Clark）在上次经济衰退后开始在迪士尼乐园担任表演。她一直在公园的加州冒险乐园中扮演一系列角色-摄影者皮菲（Phiphi），信使莫莉（Molly the Messenger）和狗女郎唐娜（Donna the Dog Lady）-每周几次，每天进行六次表演。
 克拉克女士说：“这是我一生中第一次获得安全保障。”这也是她第一次有了健康保险，带薪病假和假期。
  克拉克女士说：“我有失业和大方的家庭，”她解释说她如何继续支付房租和伙食费。
 许多表演者都依赖慈善。自大流行开始以来，演员服务基金会（Actors Fund）就是一家艺术服务机构，已筹集并分发了1800万美元的基本生活费，共计14,500人。
 “我进入演员基金会已有36年了，”首席运营官Barbara S. Davis说。 “在9月11日到2008年的卡特里娜飓风中，行业停产。显然没有什么可以比拟的。”
 高薪的电视和电影演员有更多的缓冲，但他们也遭受了失望和失去的机会。当大流行停止拍摄时，杰克·卡特莫尔-斯科特（Jack Cutmore-Scott）和现任妻子梅汉·拉特（Meaghan Rath）刚被选入CBS的新飞行员“陪审团”。
 Cutmore-Scott先生说：“我已经穿好了服装，下周我们就要去桌子上读书，但我们从来没有做过。”经过几次延期后，他们在9月听说CBS完全退出了困境。 
许多现场表演者都在寻找新的艺术途径，转向视频，流媒体和其他平台。卡拉·戈弗（Carla Gover）的舞蹈和阿巴拉契亚传统音乐的舞蹈之旅以及她创作的民间歌剧“玉米面包和玉米粉薄烙饼”都被取消了。戈弗女士说：“我经历了一段漫长的黑暗夜晚，试图设想我能做什么，”戈弗女士说，他住在肯塔基州列克星敦，有三个孩子。
 她开始每周向所有联系人发送电子邮件，共享视频并提供关于扁平足跳舞和堵塞的在线课程。反应热烈。 Gover女士说：“我弄清楚了如何使用主题标签，现在我有了新的业务。”
 但是，如果技术使某些艺术家能够分享他们的作品，则不一定能帮助他们赚到很多甚至任何钱。
 小提琴家Koh女士以致力于推广新艺术家和音乐而闻名，她贡献自己的时间来创建“ Alone Together”项目，筹集捐款以委托作曲，然后从自己的公寓通过Instagram进行表演。
 该项目广受赞誉，但正如Koh女士所说，它没有产生收入。
 Koh女士坚持说：“我很幸运。”与她的许多朋友和同事不同，她在新学校任教，因此设法保留了自己的健康保险，并且直到3月，她的抵押贷款都得到了宽恕。许多活动也已重新安排-直到2022年。
 她列出了不得不搬出家园或失去健康保险，收入以及几乎所有工作的朋友和同事的清单。 
她说：“这只是在破坏领域。” “当我展望未来时，这关系到我。”
note_en:
In the top echelons of classical music, the violinist  Jennifer Koh is by any measure a star.
 With a dazzling technique, she has ridden a career that any aspiring Juilliard grad would dream about — appearing with leading orchestras, recording new works, and performing on some of the world’s most prestigious stages.
 Now, nine months into a contagion that has halted most public gatherings and decimated the performing arts, Ms. Koh, who watched a year’s worth of bookings evaporate, is  playing music from her living room and receiving food stamps.
  Pain can be found in nearly every nook of the economy. Millions of people have  lost their jobs and tens of thousands of businesses have closed since the coronavirus pandemic spread across the United States. But even in these extraordinary times, the losses in the performing arts and related sectors have been staggering.
 During the quarter ending in September, when the overall unemployment rate averaged 8.5 percent, 52 percent of actors, 55 percent of dancers and 27 percent of musicians were out of work, according to the National Endowment for the Arts. By comparison, the jobless rate was 27 percent for waiters; 19 percent for cooks; and about 13 percent for retail salespeople over the same period.
 In many areas, arts venues — theaters, clubs, performance spaces, concert halls, festivals — were the first businesses to close, and they are likely to be among the last to reopen.
 “My fear is we’re not just losing jobs, we’re losing careers,” said Adam Krauthamer, president of Local 802 of the American Federation of Musicians in New York. He said 95 percent of the local’s 7,000 members are not working on a regular basis because of the mandated shutdown. “It will create a great cultural depression,” he said.
 The new  $15 billion worth of stimulus aid for performance venues and cultural institutions that Congress approved this week — which was thrown into limbo after President Trump criticized the bill — will not end the mass unemployment for performers anytime soon. And it only extends federal unemployment aid through mid-March.
 The public may think of performers as A-list celebrities, but most never get near a red carpet or an awards show. The overwhelming majority, even in the best times, don’t benefit from Hollywood-size paychecks or institutional backing. They work season to season, weekend to weekend or day to day, moving from one gig to the next.
 The median annual salary for full-time musicians and singers was $42,800; it was $40,500 for actors; and $36,500 for dancers and choreographers, according to a National Endowment for the Arts analysis. Many artists work other jobs to cobble together a living, often in the restaurant, retail and hospitality industries — where work has also dried up.
 They are an integral part of local economies and communities in every corner of rural, suburban and urban America, and they are seeing their life’s work and livelihoods suddenly vanish.
  “We’re talking about a year’s worth of work that just went away,” said  Terry Burrell, whose touring show, “ Angry, Raucous and Gorgeously Shameless,” was canceled. Now she is home with her husband in Atlanta, collecting unemployment insurance, and hoping she won’t have to dip into her 401(k) retirement account.
 Linda Jean Stokley, a fiddler and part of the Kentucky duo  the Local Honeys with Montana Hobbs, said, “We’re resilient and are used to not having regular paychecks.” But since March hardly anyone has paid even the minor fees required by their contracts, she said: “Someone owed us $75 and wouldn’t even pay.”
  Then there’s  Tim Wu, 31, a D.J., singer and producer, who normally puts on around 100 shows a year as  Elephante at colleges, festivals and nightclubs.
 He was in Ann Arbor, Mich., doing a sound check for a new show called  “Diplomacy” in mid-March when New York shut down. Mr. Wu returned to Los Angeles the next day. All his other bookings were canceled — and most of his income.
 Mr. Wu, and hundreds of thousands of freelancers like him, are not the only ones taking a hit. The broader arts and culture sector that includes Hollywood and publishing constitutes an  $878 billion industry that is a bigger part of the American economy than sports, transportation, construction or agriculture. The sector supports 5.1 million wage and salary jobs,  according to the U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis. They include agents, makeup artists, hair stylists, tailors, janitors, stage hands, ushers, electricians, sound engineers, concession sellers, camera operators, administrators, construction crews, designers, writers, directors and more.
 “If cities are going to rebound, they’re not going to do it without arts and cultural creatives,” said  Richard Florida, a professor at the University of Toronto’s Rotman School of Management and School of Cities.
  This year,  Steph Simon, 33, of Tulsa, finally started working full time as a hip-hop musician after a decade of minimum-wage jobs cleaning carpets or answering phones to pay the bills.
 He was selected to perform at the South by Southwest festival in Austin, Texas, played regular gigs at home and on tour, and produced “ Fire in Little Africa,” an album commemorating the  1921 massacre of Black residents of Tulsa by white rioters.
 “This was projected to be my biggest year financially,” said Mr. Simon, who lives with his girlfriend and his two daughters, and was earning about $2,500 a month as a musician. “Then the world shut down,” he said.
 A week after the festival was canceled, he was back working as a call center operator, this time at home, for about 40 hours a week, with a part-time job at a fast-food restaurant on the weekends.
  Performers on payrolls have suffered, too. With years of catch-as-catch-can acting gigs and commercials behind her, Robyn Clark started working as a performer at Disneyland after the last recession. She has been playing a series of characters in the park’s California Adventure — Phiphi the photographer, Molly the messenger and Donna the Dog Lady — several times a week, doing six shows a day.
 “It was the first time in my life I had security,” Ms. Clark said. It was also the first time she had health insurance, paid sick leave and vacation.
  “I have unemployment and a generous family,” said Ms. Clark, explaining how she has managed to continue paying for rent and food.
 Many performers are relying on charity. The  Actors Fund, a service organization for the arts, has raised and distributed $18 million since the pandemic started for basic living expenses to 14,500 people.
 “I’ve been at the Actors Fund for 36 years,” said Barbara S. Davis, the chief operating officer. “Through September 11th, Hurricane Katrina, the 2008 recession, industry shutdowns. There’s clearly nothing that compares to this.”
 Higher-paid television and film actors have more of a cushion, but they, too, have endured disappointments and lost opportunities.  Jack Cutmore-Scott and  Meaghan Rath, now his wife, had just been cast in a new CBS pilot, “Jury Duty,” when the pandemic shut down filming.
 “I’d had my costume fitting and we were about to go and do the table read the following week, but we never made it,” Mr. Cutmore-Scott said. After several postponements, they heard in September that CBS was bailing out altogether.
 Many live performers have looked for new ways to pursue their art, turning to video, streaming and other platforms.  Carla Gover’s tour of dancing to and playing traditional Appalachian music as well as a folk opera she composed, “Cornbread and Tortillas,” were all canceled. “I had some long dark nights of the soul trying to envision what I could do,” said Ms. Gover, who lives in Lexington, Ky., and has three children.
 She started writing weekly emails to all her contacts,  sharing videos and offering online classes in  flatfoot dancing and  clogging. The response was enthusiastic. “I figured out how to use hashtags and now I have a new kind of business,” Ms. Gover said.
 But if technology enables some artists to share their work, it doesn’t necessarily help them earn much or even any money.
 The violinist Ms. Koh, known for her devotion to promoting new artists and music, donated her time to create the “Alone Together” project, raising donations to commission compositions and then performing them over Instagram from her apartment.
 The project was  widely praised, but as Ms. Koh said, it doesn’t produce income.
 “I am lucky,” Ms. Koh insisted. Unlike many of her friends and colleagues, she managed to hang onto her health insurance thanks to a teaching gig at the New School, and she got a forbearance on her mortgage payments through March. Many engagements have also been rescheduled — if not until 2022.
 She ticks off the list of friends and colleagues who have had to move out of their homes or have lost their health insurance, their income and nearly every bit of their work.
 “It’s just decimating the field,” she said. “It concerns me when I look at the future.”
title: 大量失业者的“文化大萧条”织机
title_en: A ‘Great Cultural Depression’ Looms for Legions of Unemployed Performers

note:
SpaceX建议从德克萨斯州卡梅伦县的Boca Chica发射场进行星际飞船/超重型发射任务。拟议的行动将包括亚轨道发射和/或轨道发射。该行动还将包括相关的坦克测试，静态消防车测试，垂直发射区（VLA）和太阳能发电厂的扩展以及其他基础设施的建设。
    Boca Chica发射场位于德克萨斯州卡梅伦县SpaceX拥有的土地上，靠近布朗斯维尔市和南帕德里岛市。发射场由VLA组成，由发射和着陆控制中心（LLC）控制。 VLA位于美国/墨西哥边界以北约2.2英里处，而LLCC位于美国/墨西哥边界以北约1.3英里处。国道（SH）4可以通往发射场，并在VLA附近直接终止。 LLCC由两层建筑物（称为星际之门）组成，位于VLA沿SH 4的西侧。
    完全集成的运载火箭包括两个阶段：超级重型是第一阶段（或助推器），而星舰是第二阶段。完全集成的Starship / Super Heavy运载火箭的高度约为400英尺，直径为30英尺。 Super Heavy将配备多达37架Raptor发动机，而Starship将配备多达6架Raptor发动机。猛禽发动机由液态氧和液态甲烷驱动。
      在进行超重型或星舰原型的亚轨道发射之前，SpaceX必须进行坦克测试以确保坦克的可靠性。这涉及在执行静态消防车测试之前执行压力测试，以确认运载火箭的结构完整性。在储罐测试期间，SpaceX用气体介质或液体推进剂对储罐加压。
    飞行前的操作包括演练和静态消防车测试。演练的目的是验证所有车辆和地面系统是否正常运行，以及验证所有程序均已正确编写。在完成最终系统检查后，SpaceX将在运载火箭上没有推进剂的情况下进行任务排练（称为干式服装排练），然后在运载火箭上进行带有推进剂的任务排练（称为湿式服装排练）以核实是否满载。准备就绪。除了进行彩排外，SpaceX还将进行静态消防车测试。静态消防车测试的目的是验证发动机的控制和性能。静态消防车测试与湿衣服彩排相同，只是发生了发动机点火。在静态消防车测试期间，将运载火箭发动机点火约15–30秒，然后关闭。
    SpaceX提议进行亚轨道和轨道发射。发射将在VLA进行，包括两个阶段的降落在VLA或墨西哥湾距离海岸不超过12英里的平台（驳船）上。
    SpaceX提议的与发射相关的新建筑活动包括扩大太阳能农场，在VLA上增加基础设施和设施，液态天然气预处理系统和液化器。在VLA上，SpaceX建议建造一个冗余的发射台和商品，一个冗余的着陆台，两个集成塔，坦克结构试验台和一个海水淡化厂。
note_en:
SpaceX proposes to conduct Starship/Super Heavy launch operations from the Boca Chica Launch Site in Cameron County, Texas. The proposed operations would include suborbital launches and/or orbital launches. The action would also include the associated actions of tank tests, static fire engine tests, expansion of the vertical launch area ( VLA) and solar farm, and construction of additional infrastructure.
    The Boca Chica launch site is  located on SpaceX-owned land in Cameron County, Texas, near the cities of Brownsville and South Padre Island. The launch site consists of the  VLA, which is controlled by the launch and landing control center ( LLCC). The  VLA is approximately 2.2 miles north of the  U.S./Mexico border and the  LLCC is approximately 1.3 miles north of the  U.S./Mexico border. State Highway ( SH) 4 provides access to the launch site and terminates directly adjacent to the  VLA. The  LLCC consists of a two-story building (referred to as Stargate) and is located west of the  VLA along  SH 4.
    The fully integrated launch vehicle is comprised of two stages: Super Heavy is the first stage (or booster), and Starship is the second stage. The fully integrated Starship/Super Heavy launch vehicle will be approximately 400 feet tall and 30 feet in diameter. Super Heavy will be equipped with up to 37 Raptor engines and Starship will employ up to six Raptor engines. The Raptor engine is powered by liquid oxygen and liquid methane.
      Prior to conducting a suborbital launch of a Super Heavy or Starship prototype, SpaceX must conduct tank tests to ensure the tank's reliability. This involves performing pressure tests prior to performing a static fire engine test to confirm the structural integrity of the launch vehicle. During tank tests, SpaceX pressurizes the tank with gaseous media or liquid propellants.
    Pre-flight operations include mission rehearsals and static fire engine tests. The goal of mission rehearsals is to verify that all vehicle and ground systems are functioning properly, as well as to verify that all procedures are properly written. After final systems checkout, SpaceX would conduct a mission rehearsal without propellants on the launch vehicle (referred to as a dry dress rehearsal), followed by a mission rehearsal with propellants on the launch vehicle (referred to as a wet dress rehearsal) to verify full launch readiness. In addition to conducting dress rehearsals, SpaceX would conduct static fire engine tests. The goal of a static fire engine test is to verify engine control and performance. A static fire engine test is identical to a wet dress rehearsal, except engine ignition occurs. During a static fire engine test, the launch vehicle engines are ignited for approximately 15–30 seconds and then shut down.
    SpaceX is proposing to conduct suborbital and orbital launches. Launches would occur at the  VLA and include landings of both stages at the  VLA or on a platform (barge) in the Gulf of Mexico no closer than 12 miles off the coast.
    SpaceX's proposed new launch-related construction activity consists of expanding the solar farm, adding infrastructure and facilities at the  VLA, a liquid natural gas pretreatment system and a liquefier. At the  VLA, SpaceX is proposing to construct a redundant launch pad and commodities, a redundant landing pad, two integration towers, tank structural test stands, and a desalination plant.

note:
1964年3月27日，阿拉斯加发生大地震，这场地震持续了4分38秒。这种可能引发的爆发不会再持续35年。
  2013年，我在《科学美国人》杂志上撰文谈到了一种名为Cryptococcus gattii的亚热带真菌，该真菌于1999年意外出现在西北太平洋地区数百人，宠物和海豚的肺中。尽管很少见，但可以从在树林里散步等简单的事情中捡起来，并在其他情况下对健康的人致命。
  加迪犬故事最令人惊讶和令人困惑的转折之一是，看似一次爆发实际上至少是两次，也许是三次。大约在1999年左右，在温哥华岛上出现了两株不相关的加迪梭菌，而六年后的俄勒冈州威拉米特谷出现了三株。今天，我们知道这三个是如此不同，它们可能是不同的物种。当时，专家对所有人的起源感到困惑。
  提出了许多想法，包括通过风，海洋，动物，桉树，轮胎，板条箱或网球鞋介绍机会。大多数科学家都认为，真菌似乎早在数十年前就已进入西北太平洋地区，随后的一些干扰（也许是气候变化）引起了一连串的感染。
  现在，弗拉格斯塔夫和约翰·霍普金斯大学转化基因组学研究所的传染病科学家David Engelthaler和Arturo Casadevall（我在2013年为我的故事采访过Casadevall）提出了一个令人惊讶的假设：真菌不仅在从南美搭便车到美国太平洋西北地区，但随后发生海啸，到达了陆地。如果是的话，为什么感染不会在接下来的35年中继续袭击哺乳动物？
  去年，他们在《 mBio》杂志上描述了他们的假设，并将这两个情况联系在一起。对这三种真菌的DNA分析表明，大约70到90年前，当它们到达西北太平洋时，它们爆发了一次，这暗示了它们的共同起源。
  Engelthaler和Casadevall建议，该来源的一个候选人是1914年巴拿马运河的开放。空货船将水作为稳定压载物泵入船体。水和所有搭便车的生活通常被倒入下一个港口。隐球菌在海水中生存，而加迪梭菌可以生存至少一年。通过新运河运输的爆发可能使加迪角藻（C. gattii）多次从巴西等地带到西雅图，波特兰和温哥华附近的水域。 
如果是这样，真菌仍然需要使其降落。他们说，1964年的地震引发了巨大的海啸，甚至杀死了南加州的海滩上的人。
  自然灾害是有据可查的媒介。正如我在此处记录的那样，2011年密苏里州乔普林龙卷风爆发后，出现了一系列真菌性肺部感染。 1994年加利福尼亚州的北岭地震引发了山谷热的小规模爆发，这是另一种吸入性真菌病。海啸波涛汹涌的人们可能会继续遭受侵袭性皮肤和肺部感染，这种疾病被称为“海啸肺部”。2004年印度洋海啸和2011年日本海啸之后，海洋洪水造成的这种水传播感染就发生了。 2004年海啸的幸存者甚至遭受了加蒂梭菌的皮肤感染。
  但是，自然灾害是否可以将病原体引入新的地方，导致数十年后爆发新疾病？
  他们认为，有几条证据表明了这一点。在西北太平洋，受加迪梭菌污染最严重的森林和土壤是受海啸影响最严重的地区：低洼且靠近海洋。不过，一个例外-温哥华岛内部的艾伯尼港（Alberni Port）周围地区-受到海啸的严重打击。一股水流从进水口流到了26英尺高，冲走了55所房屋。如今，尽管该镇离海岸相对较远，但在那里却发现了大量的真菌。
  遗传数据还揭示了本世纪中叶的第二次进化爆发，随后是另一个稳定时期。在海上航行数十年后，新栗色的真菌可能已被迫迅速进化以在不仅与海洋截然不同的地方而且与原始房屋不一样的地方生存。野生变形虫（无定形单细胞微生物）捕食加迪梭菌。学会超越新的北美捕食者可能要花几十年的时间。它也可能无意中对真菌进行了训练，以逃避称为巨噬细胞的变形虫样免疫细胞，这些免疫细胞会在我们体内做同样的事情。 Engelthaler和Casadevall建议，这个学习时期可以解释海啸和爆发之间长达数十年的延迟。
  太平洋西北C. gattii最早的已知病例发生在1971年的西雅图。关于这种情况，我们一无所知，但是海啸假说将有助于解释这一离群值，因为真菌已经在岸上呆了好几年了。 1971年至1999年之间可能还发生了其他零星的感染，只是没有被发现，因为隐球菌可以在宿主中处于休眠状态。
  最后，也许是最重要的是，这一假说将有助于解释西北太平洋地区似乎无关的加迪酒花的杂烩以及它们出现的时间。如果经过数年的运输，几种菌株已经在海洋中建立了自己的地位，那么海啸可能会将它们同时冲上了数百英里的海岸。当然，必然的结果是，我们可能还有更多的“惊喜”，可能更有效地攻击哺乳动物。他们认为，在西北太平洋地区以及未受到海啸影响的港口和附近土地上进行进一步的环境测试可以帮助支持或驳斥其假设，并且相对容易做到。 
mBio论文于2019年10月发表，但对后续事件有影响。 Engelthaler和Casadevall提出，太平洋西北地区的C. gattii爆发可能是“黑天鹅”：这是不可预测的极端后果。确实，可能是许多甚至大多数爆发都无法预测。
  许多科学家认为，鉴于这种情况，2014年西非埃博拉疫情可能是不可避免的，但真正的原因是一群生病的候宰蝙蝠与儿童在空心树上玩耍的偶然机会。没有人预言墨西哥会爆发流感大流行，但是那件事发生在2009年。在美国，HIV，SARS-CoV-1和MERS冠状病毒，Nipa和Hendra病毒以及猴痘病毒的出现也同样出乎意料且未曾预料到。状态;寨卡病毒突然严重的产前影响以及最近怀疑怀疑由先前良性肠病毒D68引起的儿童脊髓灰质炎发作，以及温带地区C. Gattii的出现也无法预料。自1918年流感大流行以来，我们目前的困境可能是最大的天鹅，它本身可能出乎意料地起源于堪萨斯州。
  在预测新疾病暴发的问题上投入了大量资金，计算能力和调查资源。这些努力使我们今年失败了。创立了“黑天鹅”一词的金融哲学家纳西姆·塔莱布（Nassim Taleb）认为，对此类事件的正确反应并非试图对其进行预测。是为他们做准备。尽管在我看来，值得探究其起源，以便我们能够避免未来的灾难（取缔非法并积极起诉野生动植物的销售并减少森林砍伐似乎是显而易见的和人道的选择），但政府应该只是假设流行病和疫情是不可避免的，采取适当的行动。
  似乎我们没有先例进行昂贵的防御性投资。在加利福尼亚州，城市规划人员和工程师知道会发生大地震，但他们并不担心细节。毕竟，即使在研究金州地震学和地质学一个多世纪之后，1994年发生的破坏性北岭地震也发生在断层上，甚至没有出现在地震地图上。相反，它们只是相应地构建。
note_en:
The  great Alaska earthquake lasted four minutes and 38 seconds when it struck on March 27, 1964. The outbreak it may have seeded wouldn’t strike for another 35 years.
  In 2013,  I wrote in  Scientific American about a subtropical fungus called  Cryptococcus gattii that appeared unexpectedly in 1999 in the lungs of hundreds of humans, pets and porpoises in the Pacific Northwest. Although rare, it could be picked up from something as simple as a walk in the woods and prove fatal in otherwise healthy individuals.
  One of the most surprising and puzzling twists of the  C. gattii story was that what appeared to be one outbreak was actually at least two and maybe three. Two unrelated strains of  C. gattii appeared around 1999 on Vancouver Island, while a third emerged six years later in Oregon’s Willamette Valley. Today we know the three are so different they may be separate species. At the time, experts were puzzled about the origins of all.
  Many ideas were floated, including chance introduction by wind, ocean, animals, eucalyptus trees, tires, crates or tennis shoes. Most scientists agreed that the fungi seemed to have made their way to the Pacific Northwest many decades prior, and some subsequent disturbance—perhaps climate change—generated a burst of infections.
  Now David Engelthaler and Arturo Casadevall, infectious disease scientists at the Translational Genomics Research Institute in Flagstaff and Johns Hopkins University (I interviewed Casadevall for my 2013 story), have suggested a surprising hypothesis: that the fungi not only hitchhiked on ships from South America to the Pacific Northwest, but then surfed a tsunami to reach land. Y if so, why would infections not strike mammals for another 35 years?
  Describing their hypothesis in the journal  mBio last year, the pair stitch together a circumstantial case. DNA analyses of all three fungi suggest a burst of evolution when they arrived in the Pacific Northwest around 70 to 90 years ago, hinting at a common origin.
  One candidate for that origin, Engelthaler and Casadevall suggest, is the 1914 opening of the Panama Canal. Empty cargo ships pump water into their hull as stabilizing ballast. The water—and any hitchhiking life—is often dumped in the next port.  Cryptococcus species survive in seawater, and  C. gattii can survive for at least a year. The burst of shipping through the new canal may have brought  C. gattii repeatedly from a place like Brazil to the waters off Seattle, Portland and Vancouver.
  If so, the fungus still needed to make it on to land. The 1964 earthquake—which generated a tsunami so large it killed people on beaches as far south as California—seems like it could have done the job, they say.
  Natural disasters are well-documented  vectors. A burst of fungal lung infections followed the 2011 Joplin, Mo., tornado,  as I documented here. The 1994 Northridge earthquake in California  sparked a mini-outbreak of Valley fever, another inhaled fungal disease. People roughed up by tsunami waves may go on to suffer invasive skin and lung infections, a condition called “tsunami lung,” and such waterborne infections from ocean flooding occurred after both the 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami and the 2011 Japanese tsunami. A survivor of the 2004 tsunami even suffered a skin infection from  C. gattii.
  But could a natural disaster introduce a pathogen to a new place, resulting in the outbreak of a new disease decades later?
  Several lines of evidence suggest so, the pair argue. The forests and soils most heavily contaminated with  C. gattii in the Pacific Northwest are those most affected by the tsunami: low-lying and close to the ocean. One exception—the area around Port Alberni in interior Vancouver Island—was nonetheless hard hit by the tsunami. A surge of water traveled down an inlet where it reached 26 feet high and washed away 55 homes. Today the fungus is found abundantly there, even though the town is relatively far from the coast.
  The genetic data also reveal a second burst of evolution midcentury followed by another period of stability. After decades at sea, newly marooned fungi may have been forced to evolve quickly to survive in a place not only vastly different from the ocean but also dissimilar to their original home. Wild amoebas—amorphous single-celled microbes—prey on  C. gattii. Learning to outsmart their new North American predators may have taken several decades. It may also have inadvertently trained the fungi to evade the amoeba-like immune cells called  macrophages that travel our bodies  doing essentially the same thing. This learning period could explain the decades-long delay between the tsunami and outbreak, Engelthaler and Casadevall suggest.
  The earliest known case of Pacific Northwest  C. gattii occurred in 1971 in Seattle. Nothing else is known about this case, but the tsunami hypothesis would help explain this outlier, since the fungi would have already been ashore for several years. Other scattered infections may have occurred between 1971 and 1999 and simply escaped detection, as the  Cryptococcus can go dormant in hosts.
  Finally, and perhaps most importantly, this hypothesis would help account for both the potpourri of apparently unrelated  C. gattii in the Pacific Northwest and their varied emergence times. If several strains had established themselves in the ocean as a result of years of shipping, the tsunami could have washed them ashore simultaneously across hundreds of miles of coast. The corollary, of course, is that there could be still more “surprises” in store for us, ones perhaps even more efficient at attacking mammals. Further environmental testing both in the Pacific Northwest and in ports and nearby land unaffected by tsunamis could help support or refute their hypothesis and would be relatively easy to do, they suggest.
  The  mBio paper was published in October 2019, but it has implications for subsequent events. Engelthaler and Casadevall propose the Pacific Northwest  C. gattii outbreak may be a “ black swan”: an unpredictable event of extreme consequence. Indeed, it may be that many or even most outbreaks defy prediction.
  The 2014 outbreak of Ebola in West Africa was probably inevitable given the conditions, many scientists believe, but the actual cause was the chance meeting of a group of sick migratory bats with children playing in a hollow tree. No one predicted that a flu pandemic would start in Mexico, but that happened in 2009. Similarly unexpected and unpredicted were the appearance of HIV, the SARS-CoV-1 and MERS coronaviruses, the  Nipa and  Hendra viruses and the  monkeypox virus in the United States; the suddenly severe prenatal effects of Zika virus and the recent polio-like attacks in children suspected to be caused by a previously benign  enterovirus D68 were also unforeseen, as was the appearance of  C. Gattii in the temperate zone. Our present predicament is probably the biggest swan since the  1918 flu pandemic, which itself may have originated  unexpectedly in Kansas.
  Huge amounts of money, computing power and investigation resources have been thrown at the problem of predicting outbreaks of new disease. Those efforts failed us spectacularly this year. Financial philosopher  Nassim Taleb, who coined the term black swan, argues that the proper response to such events is not to try to predict them; it’s to prepare for them. Although, in my view, it’s worthwhile to plumb their origins so we can try to avert future disasters (outlawing and aggressively prosecuting the sale of wildlife and reducing deforestation seem like obvious and humane choices), governments should just assume pandemics and outbreaks are inevitable and take appropriate action.
  It’s not as though we don’t have precedent for expensive, defensive investments. In California, city planners and engineers know giant earthquakes will strike, but they don’t worry too much about the particulars. After all, even after a century or more of studying Golden State seismology and geology, the destructive 1994 Northridge earthquake  occurred on a fault that didn’t even appear on seismic maps. Instead, they simply build accordingly.

周六，成千上万的移民被困在波斯尼亚一个破烂不堪的肮脏帐篷营地中，该国降了大雪，在寒冷的天气里，气温突然下降。
 波斯尼亚西北部利帕营地的移民用毯子和睡袋包裹自己，以防该地区与欧盟成员国克罗地亚接壤。
 本周早些时候的一场大火烧毁了比哈奇镇附近的大部分营地，国际官员和援助组织已经严厉批评该营地不足以容纳难民和移民。
 当局未能在利帕为移民找到新的住所，使约一千人陷于寒冷，没有任何设施或暖气，也没有援助组织提供的零食。
 ＆＃34;雪已经落下，零度以下的温度，没有暖气，什么都没有，＆＃34;国际移民组织驻波斯尼亚特派团团长Peter Van Der Auweraert发表了推文。
 “这不是任何人都应该生活的方式。我们现在需要政治上的英勇和行动。”
 波斯尼亚已成为成千上万希望到达西欧的移民的瓶颈。由于种族分裂国家的其他地区拒绝接受这些武器，大多数人被困在波斯尼亚西北部的克拉吉纳地区。 
欧盟已警告波斯尼亚，成千上万的移民将面临无住所的寒冷冬季，并敦促该国饱受争议的政客们搁置分歧并采取行动以容纳成千上万的未眠者。
 周六，在警察寻求维持秩序的过程中，移民挤在营地中以接收波斯尼亚红十字会提供的水和食物。一些移民戴着口罩，以保护他们免受新的冠状病毒的侵害。
 计划将移民暂时搬迁至比哈奇中部封闭的设施的计划引发了居民的抗议。
 移民没有解决的办法，将纸板放在地板上，并在Lipa营地唯一的站立帐篷内设置简易屏障，以保护个人隐私。
 有些人用湿脚踩住移民在外面点燃的小火上暖身，而另一些人则用毯子紧贴身体，以保暖。
  为了到达克罗地亚，移民经常在边界沿线的山区使用非法路线。许多人抱怨克罗地亚警察的暴力和压制。
note_en:
Hundreds of migrants on Saturday were stranded in a burnt-out squalid tent camp in Bosnia as heavy snow fell in the country and temperatures suddenly dropped amid a wintry spell of bad weather.
 Migrants at the Lipa camp in northwest Bosnia wrapped themselves in blankets and sleeping bags to protect against biting winds in the region, which borders European Union member Croatia.
 A fire earlier this week destroyed much of the camp near the town of Bihac that already was harshly criticized by international officials and aid groups as inadequate for housing refugees and migrants.
 Authorities have failed to find new accommodation for the migrants at Lipa, leaving around 1,000 people stuck in the cold, with no facilities or heating and on meager food parcels provided by aid groups.
 "Snow has fallen, sub-zero temperatures, no heating, nothing," the International Organization for Migration's chief of mission in Bosnia, Peter Van Der Auweraert, tweeted.
 “This is not how anyone should live. We need political bravery and action now.”
 Bosnia has become a bottleneck for thousands of migrants hoping to reach Western Europe. Most are stuck in Bosnia's northwestern Krajina region as other areas in the ethnically divided nation have refused to accept them.
 The EU has warned Bosnia that thousands of migrants face a freezing winter without shelter, and it urged the country's bickering politicians to set aside their differences and take action to accommodate thousands sleeping rough.
 On Saturday, migrants crowded at the camp to receive water and food provided by Bosnia's Red Cross as police sought to maintain order. Some migrants wore face shields to protect them from the new coronavirus.
 Plans to relocate the migrants temporarily to a closed facility in central Bihac have prompted protests by residents.
 Left without a solution, migrants put down carboard on the floor and set up improvised barriers for a piece of privacy inside the only standing tent at the Lipa camp.
 Some people held up their wet feet above the small fires migrants lit outside to warm up, while others held blankets tight next to their bodies for warmth.
  To get to Croatia, migrants often use illegal routes over a mountainous area along the border. Many have complained of violence and pushbacks by the Croatian police.

为了吸引外国技术人员，芬兰启动了一项为期90天的迁移计划，该计划在短短一个月内收到了来自世界各地的5,300份申请。提供的是通过在北欧国家居住90天来体验芬兰人生活的机会。
 竞选活动的负责人，来自赫尔辛基商业中心的Johanna Huurre告诉《卫报》：“我们不在许多搬迁名单的首位，但是我们知道一旦有人搬来，他们就会留下来。”
 “全球人才竞争异常激烈，因此我们必须进行创造性思考。”
   该报告补充说，大约30％的申请来自美国和加拿大。其余应用均匀分布包括50个英国人和瓦努阿图的一份申请。
 Huurre告诉《卫报》，有800名是想创办创业公司的企业家，有60名是投资者，其余的是在找工作。
   “这是一次展示芬兰的伟大运动，”芬兰商务部的Joonas Halla说。 “好的是实用的方法。这里的科技行业确实蓬勃发展-据估计，到2021年，该行业将创造50,000个新工作。我们需要人才。”
 根据该计划，成功的申请人将获得有关文件的帮助，学校的帮助以及其子女的日托，甚至具有永久居留权。
note_en:
In a bid to attract foreign tech workers,  Finland launched a 90-day migration scheme that received 5,300 applications from across the world in just a month. What's on offer is a chance to sample life as a Finn by living in the Nordic country for 90 days.
 Johanna Huurre from Helsinki Business Hub, an agency that came up with the campaign, told   The Guardian, “We’re not top of many relocation lists, but we know once people do come, they tend to stay."
 “There’s huge competition globally for talent, so we had to think creatively.”
   The report adds that about  30 per cent of the applications came from the US and Canada. The remaining applications, 'evenly spread,' included 50 Britons and one application from Vanuatu.
 Huurre told  The Guardian, '800 were entrepreneurs seeking to launch startups, 60 were investors, and the remainder were job hunting.'
   “It’s been a great campaign to showcase Finland,” said Joonas Halla, of Business Finland. “What’s good is the practical approach. The tech sector here is really thriving – by one estimate it should create 50,000 new jobs in 2021. We need the talent.”
 Under the scheme, successful applicants will get help with documentation, help with schools and day care for their kids, even with permanent residency.

Facebook在唐纳德·特朗普（Donald Trump）的华盛顿经历了大量的政治审查。在乔·拜登时代，它面临的形势甚至更糟。
 从国会山的议员到联邦贸易委员会的反托拉斯执法者，华盛顿都在前所未有地了解世界上最大的社交网络。拜登的反托拉斯执法人员将拥有联邦贸易委员会本月提起的诉讼的所有权，该诉讼威胁要拆除这家庞大的公司。他的工作人员将与国会领导人商讨立法提案，这些国会领导人因不当使用用户的个人数据，散布仇恨言论和危险虚假行为而对Facebook施加了重击。
  这是立法和监管动荡的历史性时刻，对Facebook及其硅谷的弟兄们产生了深远的影响。特朗普时代为Facebook批评者打开了闸门，Facebook批评者一方面指责世界上最大的社交网络使保守派沉默，另一方面又教a了关于美国大选的虚假信息。现在，在拜登（Biden）的领导下，该公司的批评者看到了最终驯服Facebook的绝佳机会-为了数字时代的选举诚信，隐私和公平竞争。
 民主党人广泛指责Facebook领导人允许虚假信息安抚特朗普及其共和党盟友。拜登的竞选代表也对Facebook表示谴责，他们选择不从其网页上删除特朗普的误导性言论，并在紧接11月3日选举前后的几天中广泛停止政治广告。
 Open Markets Institute的执行策略主管Sally Hubbard说：“这不是惹恼即将上任的总统的好商业策略，该公司倡导对Facebook，Google和其他大型科技公司实施反托拉斯法。
 她和其他技术评论家都在向拜登施加压力，要求其采取与以往政府不同的方法，并且他们已经有几个盟友在为过渡准备下个月提出建议。公共知识的基恩·金梅尔曼（Gene Kimmelman）和美国经济自由项目（American Economic Liberties Project）的莎拉·米勒（Sarah Miller）都是科技行业市场力量的经常批评者，他们分别在拜登与司法部和财政部的过渡中提供协助。
 其他过渡顾问呼吁对大型高科技公司采取更严厉的行动。布鲁金斯学会（Brookings Institution）学者为联邦贸易委员会（FTC）审查小组提供咨询意见的比尔·贝尔（Bill Baer）上个月表示，政府需要采取更大胆的反托拉斯法。他说，在执法不力的地方，国会可能有必要进行监管。 
贝尔说：“市场并不总是能够自我纠正，而且逐案执法可能不是解决问题的最佳，最有效，最迅速的方式。”
  拜登欢迎Facebook员工和反对者加入他的过渡团队，但他将面临国会同盟和进步倡导组织的压力，要求对公司采取激进的立场。在Facebook涉足多个领域的时候，这对于全球最大的社交媒体网络可能意味着真正的麻烦。
 现在当选总统的呼吁被撤销互联网行业的神圣法律责任的保护，特别是援引选举无关误传Facebook的处理。他在一月份直言不讳地说：“我从来都不是Facebook的粉丝”。在过去的选举中，这家公司的数字化影响力推动了奥巴马-拜登去白宫的机票。
 Facebook在华盛顿的许多批评家都希望这能转化为政策。 “乔·拜登（Joe Biden）明确表示，他将把工作人员放在首位，而不是像Facebook这样的富裕公司。”众议院司法反托拉斯小组委员会主席戴维·西西里（David Cicilline）（DR.I.）提议修改美国反托拉斯法，在一份声明中说。
 “我不仅相信拜登政府不仅会采取行动使Facebook承担责任，而且我相信我们正在按照反托拉斯小组委员会的建议恢复数字市场竞争，”西西里恩继续说道。在竞选期间为拜登
 希西林（Cicilline）在对Facebook的敌意中绝不是孤单。自四年前拜登（Biden）离开公职以来，华盛顿对科技的态度已发生变化。奥巴马政府定期利用硅谷的大脑来寻求新的想法和人才，并在迅速增长的时期使公司相对不受监管。奥巴马总统甚至于2011年在Facebook总部主持了一个市政厅。
 任何行政措施或立法都可能解决整个行业的问题，而不是专门针对Facebook-采取与特朗普政府不同的，可能更成功的对策，而特朗普政府在针对特定公司的许多不满中受到挫败 
“系统中的制衡机制甚至使白宫很难惩处单个公司，正如我们在特朗普政府中看到的那样，白宫一再（但在很大程度上是失败的）努力使用政策工具对付亚马逊和TikTok谋取政治利益。”前Facebook公共政策总监马特·佩罗（Matt Perault）说，他现在领导杜克大学的科学技术政策中心。
 他继续说：“但是，拜登团队对Facebook的怀疑可能会导致司法部和FTC进行反托拉斯审查的可能性更大。” “而且拜登白宫有可能利用他们的霸道讲台，试图迫使他们通过行政行动或立法无法实现的变革。”
 Facebook的批评者说，时间已经揭示了该公司利润丰厚的商业模式的底蕴：免费向用户提供其社交网络，然后收集其个人数据，以内容和广告为目标。他们说，它为错误的信息和阴谋论提供了氧气，并创造了可以激怒某些用户的回声室。
 Facebook已采取措施在其平台上标记错误信息，或在某些情况下将其完全删除。自大选以来的几周内，该公司一直特别关注标记与选举有关的虚假信息，包括特朗普对选举操纵的虚假主张，尽管批评人士指出，该公司仍允许该内容传播给用户。
 但是在华盛顿，批评家越来越多地吸引了各个政治领域的听众。面向Facebook的Animus是中间派人士和进步的民主党人的统一点，他们已经与即将上任的政府计划采取的其他政策发生冲突。
 “要比Facebook更好的公司，因为每个人都知道自己是谁，他们很大，所以他们看起来像是垄断者？”信息技术和创新基金会主席罗布·阿特金森（Rob Atkinson）说，该组织反对FTC对Facebook提起的诉讼。 “他们将在拜登政府中面临挑战，该政府将不得不向左示善意，‘嘿，我们正在做点什么。’”
 共和党人也对Facebook处理政治言论感到不安，有人说Facebook缺乏有意义的竞争，因此可以利用它来审查用户的政治观点。在联邦贸易委员会和州检察长本月宣布他们的Facebook诉讼之后，过道两旁的议员都表示了支持。

 特朗普本人虽然是社交媒体上的多面手，但一直在抨击Facebook和Twitter，以核实这些帖子。他通过指示联邦贸易委员会（FTC）和美国联邦通信委员会（FTC）的行政命令消除了对这些公司的愤怒，该命令旨在限制第230节的范围，该节是1996年的法律，旨在保护互联网公司免于用户生成内容的法律责任。总统还多次呼吁国会废除这项法律，甚至在周三否决了一项重要的国防开支法案，因为议员们未能接受他的要求。
 但是两党对技术的挫败尚未意味着立法者将搁置党派分歧。举例来说，双方都对Facebook，Twitter和Google拥有的YouTube如何监管政治内容感到沮丧，但民主党人希望更多的节制，而共和党人则要求更少。
 参议院反托拉斯小组委员会主席迈克·李（R-Utah）参议员在一份声明中说：“我很想知道拜登政府是否会受到民主党内部要求进一步审查保守派观点的人的影响。” 。
 即使存在这种分歧，对Facebook的普遍敌意也可以帮助反Facebook的拥护者在新政府中获得吸引力。他们在就职典礼之前努力推进议程。
 “拜登-哈里斯政府可以而且应该做的事超出诉讼范围，这通常需要花费数年的时间才能解决，”社会正义组织Color of Change总裁拉沙德·罗宾逊（Rashad Robinson）说。 “这只是打破大科技公司权力集中之路的漫长开端。”
 最终，拜登填写内阁关键职位的方式，包括司法部反托拉斯部门和联邦贸易委员会的负责人，将表明他的政府计划如何处理有关科技行业竞争的问题。拜登（Biden）过渡小组拒绝对此事发表评论，但到目前为止，他们似乎在任命方面走了一条中间线-并且受到了反吹。 
拜登遭到进步派组织的抨击，该组织组成了一个名为“旋转门项目”的联盟，以反对与行业相关的候选人，他们选择了前Facebook公共政策主管路易斯·泰瑞尔担任白宫立法事务主任，并选择了前副秘书长杰西卡·赫兹（Jessica Hertz） Facebook的法律顾问，担任过渡的总法律顾问。
 与Facebook有联系的其他人也被提名为最高职位。奥巴马政府资深人士，拜登（Biden）过渡时期的联合主席杰夫·曾特斯（Jeff Zients）被任命为白宫Covid-19协调员。他此前曾在Facebook董事会任职，但据报道因与领导层意见分歧而离职。
 自首次宣布以来，在几周内，三名Facebook员工就被添加到拜登的机构审核小组中。 Facebook表示，这些员工包括公共政策执行官和副总顾问，在志愿为过渡而继续在公司工作。
 这可能为Facebook及其游说者大军提供自然的切入点，因为他们希望理顺与新政府的关系。 Facebook在华盛顿建造了一个大型的游说机器，去年花费了1670万美元，几乎是2016年的两倍。Facebook发言人拒绝对此事发表评论，也拒绝讨论Facebook对即将上任的拜登政府的影响。
 Facebook首席执行官马克·扎克伯格（Mark Zuckerberg）在上个月接受Anthony Fauci博士的采访时指出，该公司已提出支持拜登对冠状病毒大流行的反应。扎克伯格在10月还告诉国会，Facebook愿意接受社交媒体公司更高的透明度要求，以及特朗普和拜登都曾攻击的1996年技术责任法的变化。
note_en:
Facebook endured a deluge of political scrutiny in Donald Trump’s Washington. It’s poised to face even worse in the Joe Biden era.
 From lawmakers on Capitol Hill to antitrust enforcers at the Federal Trade Commission, Washington is training its sights on the world’s largest social network like never before. Biden’s antitrust enforcers will take ownership of a lawsuit the FTC filed this month threatening to dismantle the sprawling company. And his staff will negotiate legislative proposals with congressional leaders who have hammered Facebook for mishandling its users’ personal data and spreading hate speech and dangerous falsehoods.
  It’s a historic moment of legislative and regulatory upheaval with profound consequences for Facebook and its Silicon Valley brethren. The Trump era opened the floodgates for Facebook detractors, who accused the world’s largest social network of silencing conservatives on one side, and abetting disinformation about the U.S. election on the other. Now, under Biden, the company’s critics see a prime opportunity to finally tame Facebook — for the sake of election integrity, privacy and fair play in the digital era.
 Democrats widely accuse Facebook leaders of permitting misinformation to appease Trump and his Republican allies. Biden’s campaign representatives have also lambasted Facebook for choosing not to remove Trump’s misleading claims from their pages and for broadly halting political advertising in the days immediately before and after the Nov. 3 election.
 “It's just not a great business strategy to piss off the incoming president,” said Sally Hubbard, the director of enforcement strategy at the Open Markets Institute, which has advocated for antitrust enforcement against Facebook, Google and other big tech firms.
 She and other tech critics are putting pressure on Biden to take a different approach than past administrations, and they already have several allies advising the transition as it prepares to take over next month.  Gene Kimmelman of Public Knowledge and Sarah Miller of the American Economic Liberties Project, both regular critics of the tech industry’s market power, are assisting Biden with the Justice and Treasury department transitions, respectively.
 Other transition advisers have called for tougher action against the big tech companies. Bill Baer, a Brookings Institution scholar advising the FTC review team, said last month that the government needs to take bolder antitrust enforcement. And where enforcement falls short, it may be necessary for Congress to regulate, he said.
 “Markets are not always self-correcting and that case-by-case enforcement may not be the best, most efficient, and most prompt way of addressing” problems, Baer said, speaking in a personal capacity to the American Bar Association.
  While Biden has welcomed both Facebook associates and detractors into his transition team, he’ll face pressure from allies in Congress and progressive advocacy groups to take an aggressive stance toward the company. At a time when Facebook is exposed on multiple fronts, that could mean real trouble for the world’s largest social media network.
 The now-president-elect has called for the internet industry’s sacred legal liability protections to be revoked, specifically citing Facebook’s handling of election-related misinformation. He turned heads in January  when he said bluntly, “I’ve never been a fan of Facebook,” a company whose digital reach helped propel the Obama-Biden ticket to the White House in past elections.
 Facebook’s many critics in Washington are expecting that to translate into policy. “Joe Biden’s made clear that he’s going to put working people first, not wealthy corporations like Facebook,” Rep. David Cicilline (D-R.I.), who as chair of the House Judiciary antitrust subcommittee has proposed a  revamp of U.S. antitrust laws, said in a statement.
 “Not only am I confident that the Biden administration will not only take action to hold Facebook accountable, I believe we’re on track to implement the antitrust subcommittee’s recommendations for restoring competition in the digital marketplace,” continued Cicilline, who  headlined an antitrust fundraiser for Biden during the campaign.
 Cicilline is far from alone in Congress in his animosity toward Facebook. The tone toward tech has shifted in Washington since Biden left public office four years ago. The Obama administration regularly tapped Silicon Valley’s brain trust for fresh ideas and personnel, and it left companies relatively unregulated during a period of meteoric growth. President Barack Obama even   conducted a town hall at Facebook headquarters in 2011.
 Any executive action or legislation is likely to address industry issues at large rather than Facebook specifically — taking a different and potentially more successful tack than the Trump administration, which was thwarted in many of the grievances it pursued against specific companies
 “The checks and balances in the system even make it hard for the White House to punish an individual company, as we saw in the Trump administration with the White House’s repeated — and largely failed — efforts to use policy tools against individual companies like Amazon and TikTok for political gain,” said Matt Perault, a former Facebook public policy director who now leads Duke University’s Center on Science and Technology Policy.
 “But it’s certainly possible that skepticism about Facebook from the Biden team could result in a greater likelihood of antitrust scrutiny by the Justice Department and the FTC,” he continued. “And it’s possible that a Biden White House could use their bully pulpit to try to force changes that they can’t achieve through executive action or legislation.”
 Facebook’s critics say time has revealed the underbelly of the company’s highly lucrative business model: offering its social network to users free of charge and then harvesting their personal data to target them with content and advertising. They said it gives oxygen to misinformation and conspiracy theories, and creates echo chambers that can radicalize certain users.
 Facebook has taken steps to label misinformation on its platform or, in some instances, remove it entirely. In the weeks since the election, the company has been particularly vigilant about labeling election-related misinformation, including Trump’s false claims of election rigging, though critics note it still allows that content to reach users.
 But in Washington, its critics increasingly have an audience across the political spectrum. Animus toward Facebook is a point of unity among centrist and progressive Democrats, who have already been in conflict over other policies the incoming administration plans to pursue.
 “What better firm to go after than Facebook because everybody knows who they are, they're big, they appear as a monopoly?” said Rob Atkinson, president of the Information Technology and Innovation Foundation, which opposes the FTC lawsuit against Facebook. “They're going to face challenges in a Biden administration that is going to have to show its bonafides to the left that, ‘Hey, we're doing something.’”
 Republicans, too, have gripes about Facebook’s handling of political speech, with some saying its lack of meaningful competition gives it the leverage to censor users’ political views.  After the FTC and state attorneys general announced their Facebook lawsuits this month, lawmakers from both sides of the aisle expressed support.

You can unsubscribe at any time and you can contact us  here. This sign-up form is protected by reCAPTCHA and the Google  Privacy Policy and  Terms of Service apply.
 Trump himself, while a prolific poster on social media, has gone on tirades against Facebook and Twitter for fact-checking those posts. He took his anger against the companies out with an executive order instructing the FTC and Federal Communications Commission to look at limiting the scope of Section 230, the 1996 law that protects internet companies from legal liability for user-generated content. The president has also repeatedly called on Congress to repeal the law, going so far as to  veto a major defense spending bill on Wednesday because lawmakers had failed to accede to his demands.
 But bipartisan frustration with tech has yet to mean lawmakers will set aside partisan differences. Both sides have been frustrated with how Facebook, Twitter and Google-owned YouTube police political content, for instance, but Democrats want more moderation and Republicans have called for less.
 “I’m interested to see whether the Biden administration might be influenced by those within the Democratic Party calling for more censorship of conservative viewpoints,” Sen. Mike Lee (R-Utah), chair of the Senate antitrust subcommittee, said in a statement.
 Even with such divisions, the general animosity toward Facebook could help the anti-Facebook advocates to gain traction with the new administration. And they’re pushing their agenda hard ahead of the inauguration.
 “The Biden-Harris administration can and should do much more beyond the scope of litigation, which often takes years to resolve,” said Rashad Robinson, the president of social justice group Color of Change. “This is only the beginning of a long road to break up Big Tech’s concentration of corporate power.”
 Ultimately, the way Biden fills out key Cabinet posts, including the heads of the Justice Department’s antitrust division and the FTC, will indicate how his administration plans to handle questions about competition in the tech industry. The Biden transition team, which declined to comment for this story, appears so far to be walking a middle line on appointments — and getting blowback.
 Biden has come under fire from progressive groups, which formed a coalition called the Revolving Door Project to oppose industry-affiliated nominees, for selecting former Facebook public policy director Louisa Terrell as the White House director of legislative affairs and Jessica Hertz, a former associate general counsel at Facebook, as the general counsel for the transition.
 Others with ties to Facebook have also been named to top posts. Jeff Zients, an Obama administration veteran and co-chair of Biden’s transition, has been named the White House’s Covid-19 coordinator. He previously served on Facebook’s board of directors, but reportedly left amid disagreements with leadership.
 And three Facebook employees were added to Biden’s agency review teams in the weeks since they were first announced. Facebook said those employees, which include a public policy executive and associate general counsel, continue to work at the company while volunteering for the transition.
 That could give Facebook and its army of lobbyists natural entry points as they look to smooth over relations with the new administration. Facebook has built a massive lobbying machine in Washington, spending $16.7 million last year— nearly double what it spent in 2016. A Facebook spokesperson declined to comment for this story or discuss Facebook’s outreach to the incoming Biden administration.
 In an interview with Dr. Anthony Fauci last month, Facebook CEO Mark Zuckerberg noted that the company has offered to support Biden’s response to the coronavirus pandemic.  Zuckerberg also told Congress in October that Facebook is open to greater transparency requirements for social media companies, along with changes to the 1996 tech liability law that both Trump and Biden have attacked.

title_en: Will America's Next President Break Up Facebook?
